Reproductive characteristics
Male Guinea pigs are well known for their fecundity. They reach

prepuce, in connection with bilateral large fat pads.  fingers), parturition should follow within 24 hours. Normal

It is not uncommon for individuals to vary from the

Prevalence of reproductive disease
Reproductive tract disorders are a common reason for 
guinea pigs to be presented for veterinary attention. A 
retrospective review of 1000 guinea pig cases (Minarikova 
et al., 2015) indicated that reproductive tract problems 
were the third most common reason for presentation 
(15.8%), following dental disease (36.3%) and skin disease 
(33.1%).
There are significant differences in the prevalence of 
reproductive disease between sows and boars, and 
between animals of different ages. Minarikova et al. (2015) 
reported a predominance of cases were sows (94%). 
Reproductive disorders appear to be more common in 
middle-aged to old animals. Bertram et al. (2018a) reported 
that pet guinea pigs with reproductive disorders had a 
median age of 52 months, with the prevalence of these 
disorders increasing from 1.5% in animals ≤6 months of 
An ovarian cyst seen during an ovariohysterectomy as it

Chapter 18 • Reproductive disease
The rete ovarii is a network of tubules lined with vari- with cysts >1.5 cm in diameter, possibly due to compres-
ably ciliated cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells resting  sion atrophy and the resulting impaired hormonal function 
on a basement membrane. It is a normal structure in adult  of the affected ovary.
female mammals of most species. It consists of three 
parts: the paraovarian rete, the connecting rete and the 
intraovarian rete (Keller et al., 1987). These tubules begin 
in the periovarian tissue (a vestigial structure that is asso-
ciated with the ovary and consists of mesonephric tubules 
and a portion of the mesonephric duct), enter the ovary 
at the hilus and spread through the ovarian parenchyma. 
As the animal ages, the tubules begin to fill with fluid, 
resulting in cysts. The cause of this fluid accumulation 
has not yet been determined (Bean, 2013). If the cyst 
develops in the ovarian parenchyma, it is termed a cystic 
rete ovarii; if it is located in the periovarian tissue it is 
termed a paraovarian cystic rete. These cysts are thought 
to be non-functional, meaning they do not secrete hor-
mones but exert their effect by occupying space in the 
ovary and, when large enough, in the abdomen. Bertram 
et al. (2018a) questioned this paradigm, based on the high 
incidence of cystic rete and concurrent disorders of the 
uterus and other reproductive organs. Although cystic 
rete ovarii are thought to be non-steroidogenic, at the 
(a)
time of writing there has been no systematic study to 
support this suggestion.
Follicular cysts appear to develop when the concentra-
tion of luteinizing hormone (LH) is not sufficient to luteinize 
the follicle. This results in the development of a mature 
follicle that becomes cystic and may secrete oestrogen 
(Keller et al., 1987). The cause of this is unclear but it may 
relate to a dysfunction of the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovar-
ian axis in which the LH surge is either premature, absent 
or insufficient to cause follicular luteinization (Bean, 2013). 
Follicular cysts may result in irregular or persistent oestrus, 
or infertility (Keller et al., 1987).
Luteal cysts are corpora lutea that develop a central 
cavity and fail to regress. These cysts persist and serve as 
a source of progesterone. The pathogenesis of luteal cysts 
is unknown but may involve an abnormality in hypotha-
lamic–hypophyseal function or a failure of the endometrium 
to produce a luteolytic substance (Keller et al., 1987).
Guinea pigs are more likely to develop non-functional 
(b)
cystic rete ovarii than functional follicular cysts. Bean 
(2013) reviewed several studies indicating that approxi- 18.6 (a) Unilateral non-pigmented alopecia in a guinea pig that

lide injections.

biopsy specimens examined. There appears to be a correlation between the pres-
ence of ovarian cysts and uterine disease. Bertram et al.

Plain radiography is not as useful for diagnosis because of  nation (Bertram et al., 2018a). However, ovarian neoplasia

Ultrasonography can be used to better localize and char-
acterize the mass. Possible aetiologies include hormonal, 
genetic, dietary and infectious factors (Silva et al., 1998). 
See Chapter 19 for further information about neoplastic 
disease.
Ovarian teratomas are the most common ovarian 
tumours in guinea pigs (Hocker et al., 2017). They contain 
tissues of different organ types, including neural, endo-
crine, skin and muscle tissue. They are usually unilateral, 
benign and unlikely to metastasize, but may become quite 
large, up to 10 cm in diameter.
Leiomyomas (benign mesenchymal tumours) are the 
most common reproductive tract tumours in guinea pigs. 
They are usually found in animals older than 4 years of 
age and are often associated with cystic rete ovarii (Field 
et al., 1989). They are found most often in the uterus and 
occasionally in the ovaries.
Leiomyosarcomas are malignant neoplasms. They have

sistent with ovarian cysts. There may be a viscous vaginal  Leiomyoma is the most common tumour of the reproduc-

al., 2018). The diagnosis can be confirmed by ultrasono- al., 2016). Malignant neoplasms have been reported to 
graphic examination. metastasize to nearby organs (Lukášová et al., 2017).
Medical treatment has been described in other animal 
species but not validated in guinea pigs (Kohutova et al., 
2018). Ovariohysterectomy is curative and should be per-
formed rather than ovariectomy alone.
Pyometra: Pyometra is occasionally seen in guinea pigs, 
often secondary to endometrial changes as described for 
cystic endometrial hyperplasia (see above). The reported 
incidence is low, with Bertram et al. (2018a) identifying it 
in 0.7% of 709 sows. Pyometra often develops a few weeks 
after oestrus or parturition (Kondert and Mayer, 2017).
Local changes within the uterine wall, as described 
above for cystic endometrial hyperplasia, can provide a 
suitable environment for the establishment of opportun-
istic infections. Bordetella bronchiseptica, haemolytic 
Streptococcus spp., Escherichia coli, Corynebacterium 
pyogenes and Staphylococcus spp. have been identified 
in the uteri of affected guinea pigs (Kohutova et al., 2018).
Clinical signs include non-pruritic alopecia (associated 
with ovarian cysts), a mucoid or purulent vaginal discharge 
and weight loss. Abdominal palpation may reveal ovarian 
cysts and enlarged uterine horns. Ultrasonography can 
confirm the diagnosis. Ovariohysterectomy is the treat-
ment of choice. Medical treatment with two injections of 
10 mg/kg aglepristone given 24 hours apart, combined  18.9 A uterine adenoma following surgical removal.
with appropriate antibiotic therapy and then a third injec-
tion of aglepristone on day 8 of treatment, has been

Other diseases
Vaginitis/vulvitis: Wet, soiled bedding can adhere to and 
contaminate the vulva, resulting in secondary infection. 
The condition can be corrected by gently soaking the 
affected region in a dilute chlorhexidine solution and care-
fully removing the debris. If indicated, systemic antibiotics 
can be given. Vaginitis/vulvitis is easily prevented by the 
use of appropriate sanitation and good husbandry prac-
tices (O’Rourke, 2004).
Pregnancy toxaemia (ketosis): Ketosis is a metabolic 
disorder that is often triggered by anorexia in animals fed 
high-energy diets. Although it is mostly described in preg-
nant sows, in which it is termed pregnancy toxaemia, it 
can also occur in boars and non-pregnant sows. It is most 
common in the last 2 weeks of gestation and the first 1–2 
A mass in the body of the uterus, associated with an  weeks after parturition (Bishop, 2002). Obesity, lack of 
18.8
ovarian cyst. exercise, large fetal load and primiparity, a change in diet

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Treatment of ketosis can be attempted by administering 
intravenous or intraosseous fluids (5–10% dextrose), oral 
glucose, assisted feeding, calcium gluconate (given orally 
or by injection), magnesium sulphate and short-acting 
corticosteroids (Bishop, 2002). However, despite intensive 
efforts, treatment is usually unsuccessful. Prevention 
strategies entail ensuring that animals do not develop 
obesity, and avoiding abrupt changes in their diet or envi-
ronment and other sources of stress. Additionally, in 
pregnant animals, increased supplementation of higher 
energy feeds 2 weeks before parturition, to avoid a reduc-
tion of energy intake due to their restricted capacity to 
ingest food, and encouragement of exercise can be ben-
eficial (Schmid et al., 2020).
Abortion: Fetal death in the first trimester of gestation 
usually results in resorption and may be mistaken for 
unsuccessful breeding. Fetal death after the first 
Haemorrhagic vulvar discharge can be associated with  trimester usually results in abortion (Bishop, 2002). 
18.10
uterine neoplasia or pyometra. When more than one animal is affected, an infectious 
cause should always be suspected (Kondert and Mayer, 
2017) and the aborted fetuses should be submitted for 
bacterial culture and histopathology to determine the 
cause of abortion. Primary infection with Erysipelothrix 
rhusiopathiae has been documented to cause abortion 
in a guinea pig (Okewole et al., 1989), as has infection 
with Toxoplasma gondii (Green and Morgan, 1991). Other 
bacteria that have been implicated in abortion include 
Campylobacter jejuni, Salmonella spp. and Listeria 
monocytogenes (Kondert and Mayer, 2017). Stress, 
trauma, hyperthermia, ingestion of toxic plants, certain 
medications or severe dietary deficiencies can also lead 
to abortion. Secondary uterine infections are more likely 
to occur in cases of abortion than in resorption (Bishop, 
2002).
Dystocia: Dystocia is more common in guinea pigs than
